our_argument_summary: Our review says the ~12 Hz region has lower spectral power density than Schumann peaks because it lies between cavity harmonics (~7.83 Hz fundamental, ~14.3 Hz first harmonic). It is an inter-harmonic power minimum predicted by standard spherical Earth-ionosphere cavity physics, not a prominent peak. Cites Chave & Jones (2012) via DOI 10.1017/CBO9781139020138. Cross-references WIN-008 for full analysis.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-009 has NO independent validation logic in the dome's monitoring code. The only telluric domain (Domain #25 in monitor.py) validates WIN-008's 11.7 Hz cutoff, not WIN-009's ~12 Hz peak. WIN-009 appears only as a static 'confirmed' entry in the dome's confirmed_wins registry with data_source='Various' and no computation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely hardcoded/declared. No script computes or validates a 12 Hz peak from any dataset. The 'confirmed' status is asserted by fiat.
data_sources_used: None fetched for WIN-009. Domain 25 (WIN-008 only) cites 'IRIS / Geometrics MT data' but observed value is also hardcoded.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None — the WIN is never tested.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: WIN-008's detail_evidence now correctly notes Domain #25 is hardcoded with 0.0% error and pass=True — this was added since Cycle 1. WIN-009's detail text still doesn't mention the code-level non-validation, but this is less critical since the scientific rebuttal is strong and the cross-reference to WIN-008 covers it indirectly.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-009 has no dedicated domain in monitor.py. The telluric domain (#25) only covers WIN-008. WIN-009 is declared confirmed via static registry entry. Tagging 'hardcoded' rather than 'none' because WIN-009 is bundled with WIN-008's telluric domain conceptually — the dome treats them as a pair, and Domain 25's hardcoded 11.7 Hz check implicitly 'covers' both. Either 'hardcoded' or 'none' would be defensible; 'hardcoded' is the current tag and consistent with WIN-008's treatment.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The ~12 Hz inter-harmonic minimum is a well-known feature of the spherical Earth-ionosphere cavity Schumann resonance spectrum, documented in Chave & Jones (2012) and standard ELF/MT references. The dome model relabels this standard physics feature as a 'disc resonance' peak. No different numerical value is predicted.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Schumann resonances and their inter-harmonic structure were first described by Schumann (1952) and extensively characterized by Balser & Wagner (1960s). The dome model adopted the known spectral structure decades after these measurements. The dome's claim of a '~12 Hz peak consistent across independent datasets' cites no specific datasets or discovery dates.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from dome geometry produces a ~12 Hz prediction. The dome site merely states 'Multiple independent MT literature sources. Telluric ~12 Hz peak consistent across independent datasets' — a claim about existing data, not a derived prediction from disc_radius, firmament_height, or any dome parameter.

holes_found.description: The summary-table finding says 'a trough predicted by spherical-cavity physics' which is accurate but could be more precise. The ~12 Hz region isn't exactly at the deepest point of the trough — it sits on the low-frequency wing of the second Schumann mode (~14.3 Hz). Saying 'trough' is slightly loose; 'inter-harmonic minimum' (as used in the detail text) is better. However, the summary table is constrained for space and the characterization is directionally correct — 12 Hz is in the minimum region between modes, not at a peak.
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed — the summary table correctly conveys the essential point. The detail text uses the more precise 'inter-harmonic power minimum' phrasing. This is a minor precision note, not an actionable fix.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence says 'lower spectral power density than the Schumann peaks' — this is true but slightly understates the argument. It's not just lower than the peaks; 12 Hz is lower than ANY Schumann mode because it falls between all of them. A dome defender might counter 'well, it's still a local peak even if lower than 7.83 Hz' — our text should be clear that 12 Hz is on a slope (the rising edge toward 14.3 Hz), not at any kind of local maximum.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding a phrase like 'and falls on the rising slope toward the 14.3 Hz first harmonic — no local maximum exists at 12 Hz' to the detail_evidence. This would close the 'local peak' counterargument. Low priority.

holes_found.description: The dome's claim source says 'Multiple independent MT literature sources' but names none. Our review doesn't call out this absence of citations. For a claim labeled 'Literature Peak,' the dome should be able to cite specific MT papers showing a 12 Hz peak. The absence of any specific citation from the dome weakens its claim and is worth noting.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding a brief note like 'The dome cites no specific MT datasets or publications documenting this claimed peak.' This was flagged in Cycle 1 as a stronger argument opportunity but hasn't been incorporated.

stronger_arguments.description: Cycle 1 suggested noting that the dome's own code provides no validation for WIN-009 — it's declared confirmed without computation. This hasn't been added to WIN-009's detail text, though WIN-008 now mentions Domain #25's hardcoded nature. Adding even one sentence to WIN-009 ('The dome's own monitoring code contains no validation logic for this WIN — it is declared confirmed by static registry entry') would strengthen the argument by showing the dome can't even point to its own automation producing this result.

kernel_of_truth.description: The ELF spectrum around 10-14 Hz does have genuine complexity. Individual MT survey datasets can show elevated power near 12 Hz due to local conditions: cultural EM noise (power line harmonics at 50/60 Hz and their sub-harmonics), geological resonances, or the rising tail of the second Schumann mode (~14.3 Hz). Someone examining a particular MT dataset could observe features near 12 Hz that look like a peak in a limited spectral window.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The claim isn't that 12 Hz shows up in some specific dataset — it's that there's a 'prominent ~12 Hz peak consistent across independent datasets,' implying a universal feature of the telluric spectrum. The standard spherical-cavity Schumann spectrum shows this is an inter-harmonic minimum, not a peak. Local/site-specific features don't constitute a universal telluric peak, and the dome provides no mechanism for why its disc geometry would produce energy at 12 Hz specifically (no derivation from dome parameters).

fixes_applied: ISS-154 (DOI fixed): Old DOI 10.1007/978-3-540-71031-4 replaced with correct 10.1017/CBO9781139020138 (Chave & Jones, 2012). VERIFIED CORRECT via web search — resolves to 'The Magnetotelluric Method: Theory and Practice', Cambridge University Press.
ISS-155 (terminology fixed): 'Attenuation valley' replaced with 'inter-harmonic power minimum'. Verified in current text.
ISS-156 (MT dead band conflation fixed): Removed claim about geophysicists 'deliberately avoiding' 11-12 Hz range. Current text makes no such claim. Verified.
fixes_not_applied: Stronger argument about dome code not validating WIN-009 — not incorporated into detail text (suggested in Cycle 1, still relevant but low priority since WIN-008 cross-reference covers it indirectly)
Absence of dome citations for the claimed 'literature peak' — not noted in our text
cycle_1_comparison.text_change_details: Detail evidence was substantially rewritten between cycles. Cycle 1 text discussed 'attenuation valley' and geophysicist avoidance; current text is cleaner and more scientifically precise with 'inter-harmonic power minimum' framing. DOI corrected. All three Cycle 1 fixes confirmed applied.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 review of WIN-009 finds the argument in significantly better shape than Cycle 1. All three issues identified in the first pass (incorrect DOI, imprecise 'attenuation valley' terminology, conflation with MT dead band) have been fixed. The current text is scientifically precise: it correctly identifies the ~12 Hz region as an inter-harmonic power minimum between the Schumann fundamental (~7.83 Hz) and first harmonic (~14.3 Hz), cites the correct reference (Chave & Jones 2012 via verified DOI), and makes no overstated claims about geophysicist practices.

The verdict of 'Refuted by Data' holds firmly. The dome claims a 'prominent ~12 Hz peak consistent across independent datasets.' The standard Schumann resonance spectrum shows this is a minimum, not a peak. The first Schumann harmonic at 14.3 Hz proves no resonance ceiling exists at 11.7 Hz (the related WIN-008 claim). The dome's own code provides no validation — WIN-009 is declared confirmed by static registry entry without computation.

The code_analysis tags are all correct: monitoring=hardcoded (no independent domain, bundled with WIN-008's static Domain 25), relabels_standard=true (inter-harmonic structure is standard spherical-cavity physics), post_hoc=true (Schumann modes characterized since 1950s-60s), derives_from_dome=false (no derivation from dome parameters).

Remaining minor opportunities: (1) noting the dome cites no specific datasets for its 'literature peak' claim, (2) adding a sentence about the dome's code not validating WIN-009, (3) clarifying that 12 Hz is on a slope toward 14.3 Hz rather than at any local maximum. None of these rise above minor severity — the argument is solid as-is.

Confidence increased from 0.82 (Cycle 1) to 0.92 (Cycle 2) reflecting the applied fixes.

citations_verified: DOI 10.1017/CBO9781139020138 — VERIFIED via web search. Resolves to Chave & Jones (2012), 'The Magnetotelluric Method: Theory and Practice', Cambridge University Press. Correct reference for magnetotelluric spectral analysis.
citations_unchecked: Cross-reference to WIN-008 (#win008) — verified target exists and is consistent
